Quote: I kept having this feeling on Sunday nights – like this dread. Not about Monday specifically, just about the pattern.
Is this really what the next thirty years looks like?
If something makes me feel flat for more than a few months, it's not a phase, it's a signal.
I kept ignoring it because the job checked every box on paper.
I spent so long being responsible that I forgot what I actually liked.
I just sat there in his kitchen thinking, 'That's going to be me.'
Terrified. And then weirdly relieved, like when you finally say the thing you've been rehearsing in the shower for months.
My hands were shaking, but I also felt lighter than I had in a really long time.
Okay. Now what? But it was the good kind of 'now what.'
Some days I wake up at 3 a.m. doing math in my head about savings.
The discomfort of not knowing is different from the discomfort of knowing you're in the wrong place. I'd rather have the first one.
I kept thinking, 'It's just a bad week,' but the bad weeks stopped ending.
I was bargaining with myself. Like, 'If she can manage the grocery trip on her own, she's okay.'
I kept setting these little tests in my mind, and she kept barely passing them, which let me tell myself it wasn't time yet.
I think I needed it to not be time yet.
I sat in my car in her driveway afterward and just cried. Because I knew.
There's this weird mix of guilt and relief. I feel guilty for the relief, which is its own fun cycle.
It's my mom's voice, honestly. She raised us to handle things ourselves. So there's this part of me that says, 'You should have moved her in with you. That's what family does.'
I don't let guilt make my decisions anymore. Guilt is a feeling, not a plan. The plan has to work for everyone, not just the person who feels bad.
I don't want to be the person who manages it. I want to be the person who fixed it.
Managing felt like accepting defeat. Like, 'Okay, this is who I am now, pass the pills.'
I have this thing where I hate being the worst at something in public. Like, I will avoid it entirely rather than be bad in front of people.
The embarrassment lasts an hour. The regret of quitting lasts way longer.
I cried. Like, full ugly cry. My wife was there with a sign, and I just lost it.
Not because it was fast or impressive – my time was terrible – but because I genuinely didn't think I was that person.
I had this identity of myself as someone who wasn't athletic. And crossing that line broke it. That identity just… cracked open.
I catch myself now when I say 'I'm not a ____ person.' Because I said that about running for 38 years and it turned out to be completely wrong.
Is that true, or is that just the story I've been telling?
I don't like conflict. I just… went quiet. Stopped texting first. And she did the same. So we just kind of evaporated.
I was having whole arguments with her in my head. Like full conversations that never happened.
I'd be in the shower composing these devastating texts that I'd never send.
She was the person I would've called about this kind of thing.
If I feel like I was the one who was wronged, I need the other person to acknowledge it before I can move on. Like, that's my line.
I felt so ashamed that I'd made it about me. I'd spent a year being angry about something that was never about me at all.
We had this friendship where everything was easy and fun, and we didn't know how to do the hard parts. Now we're learning.
I started getting this desperation, like, 'We're never going to find anything.'
I'm not wired that way. I'm a solver. When there's a problem, I need to fix it now.
I didn't have that feeling. You know? Like, I didn't walk in and think, 'This is it.' I walked in and thought, 'This could work.' And there's a big difference.
I think I decided out loud at that table because I was tired of the conversation. Tired of being the guy who didn't have a house yet. Which is a terrible reason to make a $400,000 decision.
I make worse decisions when I feel behind.
The pressure of being the only couple in our friend group still renting was messing with me more than I realized. I was solving for my ego, not for the actual problem.
Urgency and certainty aren't the same thing.
Next time we have a big decision, if I'm feeling rushed, that's the sign to slow down.
The moments I felt most alive were when I was mentoring the new reps or when I'd have a real conversation with someone on my team who was struggling.
If you can do your job in your sleep, you're coasting, and coasting is just slow decline.
I think the brain needs to be challenged or it starts to rot. I could feel mine starting to rot.
In class discussions, I have something they don't, which is just life.
You don't owe anyone an explanation for choosing meaning over money. That's between you and your bank account.
I forgot it was an exercise. I was just… in it.
My professor stopped me afterward and said, 'You have a gift for this.' And I almost cried in front of twenty-four year olds, which would have been very uncool.
I'm a 45-year-old man who can't feed himself. That's what it felt like.
I don't let myself wallow for more than a week. After a week, you're choosing it.

Chopping an onion requires your full attention or you lose a finger. And my brain needed that. After the divorce, my mind was racing all the time – replaying arguments, second-guessing everything. Cooking was the one time my brain would shut up.
My daughter said, 'Dad, this is actually good.' And the actually should have offended me, but I was so proud.
Before, I showed love by providing, working, paying for things. Now I show love by making someone a meal. That feels more real to me.
Competence isn't fixed. You're not just born knowing things. You learn them when you have to.
She's your sister. You don't give up on family.
I was confusing not giving up with not having boundaries. Those are very different things.
You can't save someone who isn't drowning in the way you think they are.
I was treating her like a victim, and she was making choices. Bad ones, but choices. And I had to let her own those.
I will be here for her recovery, but I will not participate in her using.
I'd lie in bed, phone buzzing on the nightstand, and every part of me wanted to pick it up.
I still feel sick thinking about those nights.
I ask before I help now instead of just swooping in. That's new for me.

She told me last month that my stepping back was one of the things that made her realize she needed help. And I don't know if that's true or if she said it to make me feel better, but either way, I needed to hear it.
Monday I'm sitting next to these people joking around, Tuesday I'm supposed to evaluate their performance.
Like being an imposter, immediately.
I'd avoid giving critical feedback because I didn't want people to think the promotion changed me.
You're being liked, but you're not being respected. Pick one.
I was so focused on being the 'cool manager' that I wasn't actually managing.
There was a guy on my team who was consistently late on deliverables, and I kept covering for him because we used to eat lunch together. That's not leadership. That's people-pleasing with a title.
Be warm, be direct, don't be sorry.
I can care about someone and still tell them their work isn't meeting the bar. I don't have to apologize for holding a standard.
I grew up in a house where any criticism meant someone was mad at you. So untangling those two things – feedback and anger – that's been the real work.
All that anxiety, all those weeks I avoided this, and it took five minutes and he thanked me.
She looked resigned. Like she'd stopped hoping someone would pick her. That broke me.
I went through a period after my layoff where I felt like that – like I'd been passed over so many times that I stopped expecting anything.
She put her head on my knee and that was it. Decision made.
I research everything. I have spreadsheets for decisions. My friends joke that I'd make a pro-con list for what to eat for dinner.
The big decisions in your life, the ones that actually matter, don't come from spreadsheets. They come from a feeling in your chest that you either listen to or you don't.
I didn't want a project. I wanted a companion.

I spent my 20s and 30s trying to optimize everything. The perfect apartment, the perfect job, the perfect relationship. And it made me miserable because nothing is perfect, and optimizing is just another word for never being satisfied.
I'm trying to let things be enough. The dog is enough. The apartment is enough.
It's not settling – it's choosing contentment over ambition for the first time, and it feels… honestly, it feels like growing up.
